coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-integrated method for visual detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jun
Guo, Shengli
Jia, Lei
Zhu, Tinghui
Chen, Xiangzhen
Zhao, Tongqian
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of “superbugs”, which can cause great panic, has been proved to be related to the pollution of antibiotics in the environment water and food. It is of great research value to develop a portable, on-site and simple method for sensitive and selective detection of antibiotics. In this work, a laponite-based dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of tetracycline (TC) was manufactured through a simple assembly process. The nanoprobe can detect TC within two concentration channel from as low as 30 nM up to 54 μM with a detection limit of 9.5 nM, which met the needs in various analytical samples and showed excellent selectivity over various interferers. More significantly, the paper-based and glove-based visual probes were devised for the detection of TC by multi-color fluorescence change. And the tetracycline was also measured in milk, honey and lake after post-treatment of the samples. Combined with smartphone chromaticity analysis application (APP), the portable and disposable detection of TC can be realized. In addition, according to the fluorescence color change of the probe corresponding to the concentration of TC, two logic gates were also designed, which provided the possibility for the application of the probe in the field of smart devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laponite
Tetracycline
Fluorescence probe
Multi-color fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TC in actual samples
The construction of paper-based nanoseneor for visual detection of tetracycline
Sensing mechanism and optimal detection conditions of the dual-channel multicolor Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe
Sensitivity and selectivity of Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe
Fluorescence stability and responsivity by the environmental temperature
Determination of tetracycline in actual samples
Visual detection of TC by functionalized test paper and glove
Application of probe luminescence in logic gate circuits

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China-Guangdong Joint Fund

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) EDS spectrum of Lap-Eu-Cit. (c) Schematic diagram of a single Laponite crystal (left) and its idealized structural formula (right). (d) PXRD patterns of Laponite (black) and Lap-Eu-Cit (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) XPS full-scan spectrum. (b) C 1s deconvolution spectrum of Laponite-Eu-Cit. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Laponite-Eu-Cit in the absence (yellow) and presence of 7 μM of TC (red). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Laponite-Eu-Cit after adding 7 μM (red), 16 μM (yellow) and 54 μM (green) of TC (Inset: schematic diagram of the fluorescence color change process. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Three-dimensional fluorescence emission spectra (a) and the fitting curve of Laponite-Eu-Cit between fluorescence intensity and TC concentrations ranging from 0 to 7 μM (b). Three-dimensional fluorescence emission spectra (c) and the fitting curve of Laponite-Eu-Cit between fluorescence intensity (the relative ratios of I520/I617) and TC concentrations ranging from 7 to 54 μM (d). (e) CIE chromaticity diagram showing the (x, y) fluorescent color coordinates of Laponite-Eu-Cit with the TC concentration ranging from 0 to 54 μM. (f) The fluorescence images of Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe in the presence of 0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 20, 22, 25 and 40 μM TC under a UV lamp at 365 nm.

(a) Visual semi-quantitation detection of TC based on test paper. (b) Standard colorimetric card for the testing of TC. (c) The function relationship between the intensity ratio (Y) of R/B and TC concentrations (C1) ranging from 0 to 20 μM (right) and the large concentration range of 20–100 μM (left).

(a) The photo of Laponite-Eu-Cit colloid after centrifugal separation. (b) The fluorescent photo of the glove-based Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe without touching cheese under a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Sliding sampling of the detection glove on the surface of cheese coated with TC (under natural light). (d) The fluorescent photo of the detection glove after touching cheese coated with TC under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Logic gate circuit diagram LG-1. (b) Column diagram of the normalized fluorescence intensity ratios of I520/I617 for detecting different amounts of TC (ranging from 0 to 54 μM). (c) Logic gate circuit diagram LG-2.
The preparation of Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe and visual detection of TC.
Determination of TC in milk, honey and lake water samples by Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe.
Truth table corresponding to the LG-1 circuit.
Truth table corresponding to the LG-2 circuit.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-integrated method for visual detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of “superbugs”, which can cause great panic, has been proved to be related to the pollution of antibiotics in the environment water and food. It is of great research value to develop a portable, on-site and simple method for sensitive and selective detection of antibiotics. In this work, a laponite-based dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of tetracycline (TC) was manufactured through a simple assembly process. The nanoprobe can detect TC within two concentration channel from as low as 30 nM up to 54 μM with a detection limit of 9.5 nM, which met the needs in various analytical samples and showed excellent selectivity over various interferers. More significantly, the paper-based and glove-based visual probes were devised for the detection of TC by multi-color fluorescence change. And the tetracycline was also measured in milk, honey and lake after post-treatment of the samples. Combined with smartphone chromaticity analysis application (APP), the portable and disposable detection of TC can be realized. In addition, according to the fluorescence color change of the probe corresponding to the concentration of TC, two logic gates were also designed, which provided the possibility for the application of the probe in the field of smart devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is one of the most widely produced and used antibiotics, which is widely used in the treatment of bacterial infections in human, animal husbandry and aquaculture [1–3]. According to a recent government survey, the annual use of tetracycline reached 5866 tons in the United States in 2016 and 6950 tons in China in 2013 [4]. Due to the relatively low rate of metabolism and absorption, most of the tetracyclines used in animal husbandry are released directly into the environment [5]. The different charged forms and easy ionization of tetracycline lead to the adsorption of tetracycline to the surface of soil minerals or organic matter by hydrophobic distribution, cation exchange, cation bond bridge, surface coordination and hydrogen bond [6,7]. At the same time, environmental factors such as environmental pH, temperature and ionic strength can affect the adsorption behavior of soil to tetracycline antibiotics [8]. It can be confirmed that a large number of tetracycline residues in the environment can be detected in water, soil and even animals and plants [5,8]. The residues can not only pose a threat to food safety, but also lead to the accumulation in the human body and the environment, thus increasing the resistance of bacteria to antibiotics [9–11]. Tetracycline antibiotics are a double-edged sword, which can kill both harmful and beneficial microorganisms, and can also produce drug resistance [12]. Bacteria can obtain antibiotic resistance gene by gene mutation or horizontal gene transfer [13]. The exchange of antibiotic resistance genes between agricultural soil bacteria and clinical pathogens is recognized a severely underestimated mechanism of antibiotic resistance spread [14]. Therefore, the development of an efficient TC monitoring platform is of great significance to improve food safety and protect human beings from antibiotic contamination.

At present, the commonly used tetracycline detection methods are high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [15], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [16], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [17] and chemiluminescence method [18], etc. However, these methods often require high-cost instruments, complex and time-consuming pretreatment of the samples (extraction and purification), and skilled technicians [19,20]. Therefore, it is of great research value to develop a portable, on-site and simple method for the detection of TC in a variety of fields.

In recent years, fluorescence-based methods have become popular methods for TC detection because of the advantages over other methods in portability, rapidity and operability [21]. A number of fluorescent probes for TC based on quantum dots and carbon dots have been reported [22,23]. Lanthanide-based probes have the advantages of large Stokes shift, narrow emission band and long fluorescence lifetime, which are of great significance in the design and application of fluorescent probes [24–27]. TC molecule has a β-diketone structure, it is easy to chelate with Eu3+, transfer its own energy to Eu3+, and make Eu3+ emit characteristic luminescence by antenna effect [28]. However, water molecules will lead to fluorescence quenching in the coordination process of TC and Eu3+, and the sensitivity of many molecular probes is relatively low [29]. To improve the sensitivity, ancillary ligands such as Triton X-100 [30], hydrogen peroxide [31], citrate [10] can be added to coordinate with Eu3+ to replace the coordinated water molecules. However, lanthanide complex-based probes often suffer from poor photostability, poor water dispersion, narrow detection range or expensive raw materials, which limit their practical application [32]. Recently, lanthanide complex-based organic-inorganic hybrid materials are emerging in a variety of applications [33]. Considering the diversity of TC concentration in different analytical samples, there is a great demand for new TC probes with low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity.

Herein, a dual-channel multicolor Laponite-based fluorescence nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity was developed by using a simple and inexpensive strategy (Scheme 1 ). Laponite (Lap), a synthetic layered silicate (chemical composition Na0.7[Mg5.5Li0.3Si8O20-(OH)4](H2O)n) with Na+ located in the interlayer, is an inexpensive and very versatile inorganic matrix for the assembly of nanoprobe [34–37]. The clay was characterized by 2:1 crystals and consisted of a layered unit in which an octahedral sheet containing Mg2+ (partially replaced by Li+) was sandwiched between two tetrahedral silica sheets [36]. The individual clay nanoparticles had a disc-like nanostructure, and the high surface charge caused the clay to disperse completely in water, resulting in a transparent solution. Through the synergistic action of ion exchange and coordination process, the citrate (Cit) ligand was loaded on the Eu3+-laponite, resulting in a water-dispersible and inexpensive nanoprobe. The nanoprobe can be used for TC detection from 30 nM to 54 μM through the change of dual-channel fluorescence, which met the needs of various analytic samples, and showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity. This proposed nanoprobe was applied in the detection of TC concentrations in milk and honey samples for practical applications. Furthermore, portable test strips and wearable devices based on dual-channel nanoprobe were also fabricated, which provided a convenient and reliable detection process for the analysis of TC in different water environments and agricultural products. In view of the excellent fluorescence color variability of the prepared nanoprobe, it can be extended to the field of logic gate devices. The logic gate switches designed with variables such as tetracycline concentration and fluorescence color can realize the qualitative and semi-quantitative intelligent monitoring of tetracycline.

Laponite RD was purchased from Beijing AiGao Technology Co., Ltd. Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and trichloroacetic acid were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Hedong District of Tianjin Hongyan Reagent Factory. Ethanol was purchased from Shanxi TongJie Chemical reagent Co., Ltd.

The total amount of 250 mg Laponite RD was dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water, and then 10 mg/mL transparent colloid solution was obtained by stirring 10 min. Next, 80 mg of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was added in the above colloid solution and the solution was stirred for 5 h at 80 °C. After the system was cooled to room temperature, the Laponite-Eu was obtained and washed with water several times by centrifugation.

Dispersing the Laponite-Eu obtained from the previous step into 20 mL ethanol, and then 5 mL ethanol solution of citric acid (30 mg/mL) was added in Laponite-Eu dispersion and stirring for 5 h at 60 °C. After the system cooling to room temperature, the Laponite-Eu-Cit was collected and washed by water by centrifugation. The final product was dried in vacuum for 24 h at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk, honey and lake samples were employed as the actual samples. The actual milk samples treatment method was referred to the previous reports [38–41]: pure milk was bought from the supermarket, and then trichloroacetic acid (300 g/L) was added to milk according to 1% (v/v). After ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, the organic molecules such as proteins and lipids were removed by centrifugation and the obtained liquid was filtered with 0.22 μM filter membrane. The pH of the system was adjusted to 8 with a small amount of sodium hydroxide (6 M). The treated clear liquid was used to prepare the milk supernatant containing the standard concentration of TC. The honey purchased in the supermarket was diluted 20 times with buffer and filtered with a 0.22 μM filtration membrane. Then the TC standard solution with various concentrations was added. The fluorescence intensity or fluorescence ratio was measured, and the concentration of TC in each sample was determined according to the standard curve. The repeatability of the detection method was investigated by repeated determination (3 times), and the RSD value was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of paper-based nanoseneor for visual detection of tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: The Laponite-Eu-Cit nanosensor was fixed on the test paper before the detection. The common filter papers were cut into thin strips, and immersed into 5 mL of Laponite-Eu-Cit aqueous solution (about 15 mg/mL). After drying, the test strips were obtained, which can be directly used for the detection of TC. Briefly, TC standard solutions (30 μL, 1–100 μM) or actual sample solution were added onto the test paper, and the change of fluorescence color of the test solution at 365 nm excitation wavelength was observed by ultraviolet lamp.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the energy dispersive spectra (EDS) obtained with a Tecnai-G2-F30 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) was carried out by a Rigaku-Dmax 2400 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation over the 2θ range of 5–70°. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed on a Bruker V70 FTIR spectrometer utilizing the standard KBr compression technology in the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron spectrometer. Contents of metal elements were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) through an IRIS Advantage ER/S spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were investigated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer) with a Xe arc lamp as the excitation source.

Laponite, a trioctahedral smectite clay with the formula of Na0.7[Mg5.5Li0.3Si8O20-(OH)4](H2O)n) was employed as a platform due to the distinguished interfacial properties [42]. The morphology (Fig. 1 a and b), idealized structure (Fig. 1c), and PXRD patterns of Lap (Fig. 1d) were performed. The negative charge on the surface of the nano-laponite disk made it easy for cationic and neutral organic molecule to be accommodated on a separate disk, which enabled Eu3+-Cit complexes to be formed in situ on the disk in aqueous solution. The fine structure of the sample was investigated by TEM (Fig. 1a), and the characteristic stacked disordered 2D sheet-like structures were observed [43]. This structure provided a large surface area and was of great help to improve the sensitivity of detection. The EDX diagram (Fig. 1b) displayed the attendance of C, O, Si, Mg and Eu elements in the nanoprobe, while that of Na element was invisible, indicating the accommodation of Eu-Cit complex on the Lap disk through the ion-exchange process [43], and the intercalation of guest molecules in the Lap intercalation usually led to the enlargement of the interlayer spacing [42]. In order to obtain accurate results, the sodium contents before and after ion exchanges were determined to 0.941% and 0.104% through ICP-AES, indicating a high yield of the ion exchange process. The PXRD patterns of the two samples (Fig. 1d) showed four reflection peaks at 6.5° for (001), 20° for (110), 35° for (130), and 61° for (060), respectively. After the accommodation of Eu-Cit complex with laponite, the (001) reflection of laponite-Eu-Cit shifted toward the lower reflection angle, which was due to the increase of the interlayer distance. In order to demonstrate the successful complexation of citric acid, the FT-IR spectra of the samples were also tested (Fig. S1). The absorption peaks at 1010 cm−1 corresponded to Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching, while the peaks at 1637 cm−1 and 3443 cm−1 corresponded to structural hydroxyls and absorbed water molecules, which were observed in all the samples [44]. The peaks appeared at 1615 cm−1 and 1428 cm−1 in laponite-Eu-Cit were asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of carboxyl groups [45], which proved the successful modification of the Eu-Cit complex.

The surface chemical composition of Lap-Eu-Cit was studied by XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ). As can be seen from Fig. 2a, the XPS full-scan spectrum of the nanoprobe contained Mg 1s, O 1s, Si 2p, C 1s and Eu 3d peaks, which were derived from laponite and Eu-Cit complex. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s was shown in Fig. 2b, and the peaks at 288.7 eV, 286.6 eV and 284.7 eV were assigned to CO, COH and CC bonds from Eu-Cit, respectively. Combined with the PXRD and FT-IR measurements, it can be inferred that citric acid was successfully accommodated with laponite [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism and optimal detection conditions of the dual-channel multicolor Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to demonstrate the detecting mechanism of the nanoprobe, the changes in fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe to different concentrations of TC was measured under the optimized excitation of 395 nm (as shown in Fig. S2), and the energy transfer mechanism diagram was shown in the Fig. 3 and Fig. S3. The Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe showed no obvious peaks in the absence of TC, due to that the europium ion was coordinately unsaturated and the fluorescence was quenched because of the present of the water molecules. With the addition of TC (7 μM), the linear emission peaks at 593, 617, 652 and 695 nm were significantly enhanced by the characteristic emission of Eu(III) ions (5D0) to the ground state (7FJ, J = 1, 2, 3, 4), with the hypersensitive transition 5D0→7F2 as the most prominent spectral line (Fig. 3a). This enhancement can be attributed to two reasons. On the one hand, TC with β-diketonate configuration can effectively absorb energy and transfer energy to Eu3+ ions through antenna effect. This can be specified by the singlet and triplet states levels of TC, which played an important role in energy transfer from TC to Eu3+ ion. As shown in Fig. S3, the singlet and triplet state energy of TC were 36200 cm−1 and 19160 cm−1 in methanol solution, respectively [47]. The triplet excited energy level of TC was about 1860 cm−1 higher than the 5D0 level of Eu3+, which was just within the range of the optimal energy level difference, resulting in the effective energy transfer from TC to Eu3+ ion. On the other hand, TC can replace the coordination water molecules and strongly coordinate with Eu3+ ions, which reduced the non-radiation quenching from the high-frequency OH oscillators [48]. To explain the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement, the number of water molecules in the first coordination sphere was examined, even at low concentration or trace amount of TC. It can be observed that the fluorescence lifetime of Laponite-Eu-Cit-TC in D2O buffer was enhanced compared to that in aqueous buffer (Fig. S4). This was because the O-D oscillator of the coordinated D2O were ineffective to deactivate excited states of Eu(III). The numbers of coordinated “H2O” in the nanomaterials can be determined according to the equation [49,50]:
q = 1.1
τ
H 2
O
- 1
-
τ
D 2
O
- 1
- 0.31
where τH2O and τD2O are the lifetimes of nanomaterials in H2O and D2O buffers, respectively. The value of q decreased from 5.12 in Laponite-Eu-Cit to 2.99 in Laponite-Eu-Cit-TC, indicating that TC can replace the coordination water molecules and inhibit vibronic coupling with O–H oscillator.

Furthermore, to study the fluorescence stability of nanoprobe for the detection of TC, kinetic scans of fluorescence induced by 450 W Xe arc lamp (395 nm) for the clay-based material Laponite-Eu-Cit-TC and isolated organic complex cit-Eu-TC were carried out. As displayed in Fig. S5, the fluorescence emission intensity of the two samples decreased with the increase of exposure time, and the decline rate of Laponite-Eu-Cit-TC was slower than that of isolated organic complex cit-Eu-TC. It was understandable that the interaction between inorganic host and organic guest might restrict the vibration of the ligand and reduce the non-radiative transition, thus improving the corresponding fluorescence stability [51].

It was worth noting that a new peak at 520 nm appeared with the increasing of TC concentrations [52], while the characteristic emission bands of Eu(III) ions (5D0) gradually decreased (Fig. 3b). To analyze the origin of the peak at 520 nm, the room-temperature emission spectra of TC, Lap, and the complex of Lap and TC were examined (Fig. S6). It can be seen that there was almost no fluorescence emission peak in a single TC or Lap solution. After combined TC with Lap, the fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm appeared. It can be attributed to the deprotonated excess tetracycline can easily coordinate with the cations in ionic laponite to form complexes under alkaline conditions, resulting in the emergence of fluorescence peak at 520 nm [53]. In addition, the characteristic emission bands of Eu(III) ions (5D0) gradually decreased. This can be due to that the addition of excessive TC changed the coordination microenvironment of Eu(III) ions [54], which increased the probability of non-radiative decay transition, resulting in ineffective energy transfer to Eu(III) ions.

In order to obtain the fast response and high sensitivity for TC detection, the reaction times and pH values were optimized. The change of the fluorescence intensity of Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe after adding TC was studied at different time intervals. The fluorescence response of the Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe to 5 μM of TC was shown in the Fig. S7a, the characteristic emission of Eu(III) ions at 617 nm reached the highest peak within 1 min, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of reaction time. With the further increase of TC concentrations, the peak at 520 nm appeared, while the characteristic emission bands of Eu(III) ions (5D0) gradually decreased. The fluorescence response ratios (I520/I617) of the Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe toward TC (26 μM) were also explored at irregular time intervals. As shown in Fig. S7b, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I520/I617) reached 0.41 in 1 min and was very stable in the next 9 min. Therefore, 1 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time.

In addition, the experimental pH was also optimized. As shown in Fig. S8, in the pH range from 4.0 to 13.0, the fluorescence intensity of Eu(III) ions (5D0) increased first and then decreased, and finally reached the maximum at pH = 8, which was consisted with the previous report [55]. Under acidic conditions, TC was easy to be protonated, which was not conducive to the effective energy transfer to Eu (III) ions. While under strong alkaline conditions, Eu (III) ions had partially formed europium hydroxide precipitation, resulting in the decrease of fluorescence intensity [56]. The above results showed that the Lap-Eu-Cit-TC coordination complex was more stable under weak alkaline conditions, so the pH value of the best detection system of TC in this study was selected as 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above optimized experimental conditions, the ability of Lap-Eu-Cit system to detect TC was determined by analyzing the variation of fluorescence intensity of probe to different TC concentration [48,55]. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , upon gradually increasing TC concentrations from 0 to 7 μM, the emission intensity at 617 nm increased continuously, and fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at 7 μM of TC (Fig. 4a and Fig. S9a). A correlation coefficient of 0.997 was obtained from the linear fitting in the range of 0 ~ 7 μM (Fig. 4b), and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 9.5 nM by the method of signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3). Then, the fluorescence emission intensity at 617 nm was downward as the TC concentration further increased, while the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm increased gradually (Fig. 4c, Fig. S9b and Fig. S10). Fig. 4d showed the ratio of the intensities at 520 nm and 617 nm (I520/I617) linearly increased with increasing TC concentrations from 7 to 54 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.987. Fig. 4e indicated the emission changes of Laponite-Eu-Cit for various TC concentrations in the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage’s (CIE) chromaticity diagram, and the corresponding color coordinates values were also provided in Table S1. The results demonstrated that when the TC concentration increased from 0 to 7 μM, the luminescence gradually convert to the red region, and when the TC concentration further increased from 7 to 54 μ M, the emission gradually changed to the yellow region. The changes of fluorescence color can be observed with naked eye (Fig. 4f), which made it possible to detect tetracycline easily and rapidly.

It was of great significance to compare this method based on Lap-Eu-Cit with other methods reported in recent years. As shown in Table S2, except for ZnO-CdS@Au nanocomposite, the detection limit of the dual-channal fluorescence Lap-Eu-Cit probe was lower than that of electrochemical analysis, electrochemiluminescence, colorimetric and single-fluorescence switch sensor, indicating that this probe had high sensitivity. However, compared with ZnO-CdS@Au nanocomposite, Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe can realize the detection of higher concentration of TC, which was highly desirable for fulfilling the requirement for safety control and food quality. In addition, the preparation and detection process of Lap-Eu-Cit was simpler than other methods, and was suitable for on-site and rapid detection.

However, there are often a large number of coexisting metal ions and competitive molecules in actual samples, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, glucose and cysteine [57]. In order to verify the feasibility of the nano-probe in practical detection, the stability and sensitivity of the nano-probe for tetracycline detection under the coexistence of metal ions and other small molecules were further studied. As displayed in Fig. S11a, there was no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of the system after the addition of interfering metal ions, indicating that Lapite-Eu-Cit was expected to be used for the identification of TC in real samples. To further examine the selectivity of Laponite-Eu-Cit prepared by this method, other antibiotics (thiamphenicol, chloramphenicol, ofloxacin and kanamycin sulfate) were also determined under optimal conditions (Fig. S11b). The results showed that the above derivatives had little effect on the fluorescence intensity, indicating the good selective recognition ability of Lap-Eu-Cit to TC. This phenomenon can be attributed to that TC with β-diketonate configuration can strongly coordinate with Eu3+ ions and effectively transfer energy to Eu3+ ions. However, other interfering molecules can not coordinate with Eu3+, so it had almost no effect on the fluorescence of Eu3+. On the other hand, it was also difficult for other metal ions to coordinate with TC competitively. Therefore, the strong binding of Eu3+ and TC resulted in a stable fluorescence emission, while other interfering substances can hardly affect the fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability and responsivity by the environmental temperature
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of the fluctuating environmental temperature on the stability of the nanoprobe, the dependence of the normalized emission intensity of 5D0→7F2 transition (616 nm) of Eu3+ at different temperatures (10, 20, 30 and 40 °C) on irradiation time were also studied. As shown in Fig. S12, the initial fluorescence intensities of the nanoprobe were hardly affected by the environmental temperature, suggesting that the nanoprobe can be used in different seasons. To further study the relationship between the temperature and the sensitivity of the probe, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of the probe at a certain concentration of TC under different temperatures (from 5 to 40 °C) were obtained. As shown in Fig. S13, the red and blue lines represent the changes in I/I0 during heating and cooling process, whereas I and I0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the probe with a certain concentration of TC under elevated temperatures and 5 °C, respectively. The relative fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 decreased with increasing temperature, which was consistent with nonradiative relaxation and thermal deactivation of the europium by the vibrational interactions with their environment [58,59]. There is a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and temperature, whether in the process of heating or cooling. In addition, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) was reversible for 5 cycles of continuous heating and cooling in the temperature range of 5–40 °C (Fig. S13), indicating that the fluorescent probe was stability for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tetracycline in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the good TC detecting performance, the Laponite-Eu-Cit nanoprobe was used to determine the TC in milk, honey and lake samples, which was of great significance to food safety and human health [60]. It can be seen in Table 1 , when the spiked concentrations of TC were 0.5, 2.5, 5.5, 10, 25 and 45 μM, the recoveries ranged from 90.13% to 105.27%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) was between 0.5% and 6.39%, demonstrating the Laponite-Eu-Cit had very good reliability and accuracy in the testing of TC in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of TC by functionalized test paper and glove
ce:section.ce:para:
Given that simplicity and rapidity are the development trend of current detection methods, an economical and efficient test paper for TC visual inspection was developed by infiltrated filter paper with Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe (Fig. 5 a). Under the help of a portable ultraviolet lamp with excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence color of the test paper changed from dark-blue to red and then turned to yellow with the increase of TC concentrations, which indicated that the change of TC concentrations could be observed by the naked eye (Fig. 5b). Therefore, using the test strip containing tetracycline standard solution as the standard colorimetric card, and comparing the color of the actual test paper with the standard colorimetric card, the concentration of tetracycline drugs can be obtained. Furthermore, the chromaticity analysis application (APP) in smartphone was used to analyze the fluorescence color of each concentration of TC on the test strip and convert the red-blue ratio (R to B) or green-to-red ratio (G to R), which ensured that the color can be digitized in the quantitative detection of TC on site. The fluorescence color change of Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe in various concentrations of TC test paper can be evaluated by comparing and calculating the intensity ratios of R/B and G/R (Fig. 5c). Moreover, there was a good function relationship between the intensity ratio (Y) of R/B and TC concentrations (C1) in the small concentrations ranging from 0 to 20 μM, and the linear equation was:
Y = 0.669 + 0.112
C 1
- 3.48 ×
10
- 3
C 1 2

In the large concentration range, a good function relationship between the intensity ratio (Y) of G/R and TC concentrations (C2) ranging from 20 to 100 μM, and the linear equation was:
Y = 0.108 + 0.0171
C 2
- 8.53 ×
10
- 5
C 2 2
The above research results showed that an effective visual testing method for TC was established, which was beneficial to the semi-quantitative detection of TC.

Since the laponite-based material was a colloidal transparent material with a certain viscosity, a glove-based wearable device was also constructed for non-invasive TC analysis by fixing Lap-Eu-Cit gel on nitrile butadiene gloves (Fig. 6 ). The colloidal Lap-Eu-Cit nanoprobe (Fig. 6a) was diluted and coated on the glove for the detection of TC. As shown in Fig. 6b, the surface of the cheese was tested by the glove probe. There was no obvious fluorescence color change on a TC non-contaminated surface in the blank experiments (Fig. 6c). In contrast, a red color was observed upon the surface of the cheese wiped by 50 μM of TC after being irradiated with a portable miniature UV lamp (Fig. 6d). Moreover, this wearable glove-based nanoprobe also showed good sensitivity in the detection of trace TC on the surface of other fruits and vegetables under portable UV lamp (Fig. S15). The surface sensing analysis of non-invasive TC was repeated for 5 times, and it had good analytical characteristics for all target surfaces. The results showed that different surface characteristics had no effect on the qualitative detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of probe luminescence in logic gate circuits
ce:section.ce:para: According to the phenomenon of discoloration of Laponite-Eu-Cit induced by TC, the nanoprobe was applied to the logic gate circuit (Fig. 7 a) for TC detection [61]. Whether TC existed as Input 1, the non-existence and existence of TC were defined as “0” and “1”, respectively. The TC concentration was used as Input 2, and the dose of 7 μM was the threshold. The normalized luminescence intensity excited at 520 nm (Fyellow) and 617 nm (Fred) were selected as the double output signal (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the output (0, 0) indicated that no red and yellow output signals were observed, and the TC concentration was 0. The output (1, 0) indicated that there was a low concentration of TC (0–7 μM), and only red emission was turned on. The output (1, 1) represented the presence of a high concentration of TC (7–54 μM), which was accompanied by the red emission and yellow emission were turned on. Different concentrations can be clearly displayed from the emission color, and the true values were shown in Table 2 . The corresponding colors of different concentrations of tetracycline were also defined, the residue without tetracycline was expressed in dark blue, with low concentration tetracycline (0–7 μM) was expressed in red, and with high concentration tetracycline (7–54 μM) was expressed in yellow.

In order to research the application prospects of automated detection of TC by the nanoprobe, a logic gate circuit (LG-2) composed of three-state gate and OR gate was also designed (Fig. 7c). The circuit LG-2 can output three kinds of signals, “0”, “1” and “Z” (high impedance), which realized the conversion between the fluorescence color input signals (EmYellow and EmRed) and the tetracycline concentration output signals. The EmYellow was represented by Input 1, and the presence and absence of yellow light were represented as “1” and “0”, respectively. The EmRed was represented by Input 2, and the presence and absence of red light were also represented as “1” and “0”, respectively. When the input was (0,0), indicating there was no tetracycline, and the output was “Z”. When the input was (0,1), it stated there was a small amount of tetracycline (0–7 μM), and the output is “0”. While there was a large amount of tetracycline (7–54 μM), the input was (1, 1) and the output was “1”. The truth table of this logic gate circuit was shown in Table 3 . In other words, the logic gate LG-2 can semi-quantitatively output the concentration of tetracycline according to the detected fluorescence color, and realize the detection of tetracycline. Input 1 and input 2 represented the fluorescence color, output Z indicated no tetracycline residue, output 0 indicated low concentration tetracycline residue, and output 1 indicated high concentration tetracycline residue.

In brief, a multi-color fluorescence nanosensor based on Lap was reported in this research, which can realize the sensitive detection of tetracycline within two concentration channel from as low as 30 nM up to 54 μM with a detection limit of 9.5 nM. Such low LOD of TC for this nanoprobe was highly desirable for fulfilling the requirement for safety control and food quality. Interestingly, tetracycline can be intelligently recognized through a test paper-based visual probe by chromaticity analysis APP on the smartphone. In addition, the sensing material can be applied to latex gloves to realize the fast detection of tetracycline residues on the surface of many kinds of food. Based on the fluorescence color transformation with the change of TC concentrations, two logic gate switches were also designed, which provided the possibility for the application of the nanosensor in the field of intelligent devices. With further optimization, this strategy can also provide promising potential in the on-site detection of a variety of environmental pollutants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51773052, No.U1704241 and No. U1904168), the Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province (194200510010), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015, No. 19HASTIT040), the Science and Technology Research Project of Henan province (No. 182102310702), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M642748).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127741.

ce:section.ce:para:
